text: {"overallConclusion":"Regular nut consumption reduces cardiovascular risk.","inferences":[{"type":"evidence-for","premiseIndexes":[0],"conclusionIndex":-1,"warrant":"Multiple large prospective cohort studies showing an association between nut intake and lower cardiovascular disease incidence suggest a causal relationship.","strength":"moderate","defeaters":["Potential confounding variables","Reverse causation","Measurement error in dietary assessment"]},{"type":"evidence-for","premiseIndexes":[1],"conclusionIndex":-1,"warrant":"A dose‑response relationship observed in the Nurses' Health Study (35% lower risk with ≥5 servings/week) strengthens the inference that nuts protect against coronary heart disease.","strength":"moderate","defeaters":["Residual confounding by lifestyle factors","Self‑report bias in nut consumption"]},{"type":"supports","premiseIndexes":[2],"conclusionIndex":-1,"warrant":"Randomized controlled trial evidence (PREDIMED) provides experimental confirmation that adding mixed nuts to a Mediterranean diet lowers major cardiovascular events.","strength":"strong","defeaters":["Trial design limitations","Population‑specific effects"]},{"type":"undercuts","premiseIndexes":[3],"conclusionIndex":2,"warrant":"Irregularities in randomization raise doubts about the internal validity of the PREDIMED findings, weakening confidence in the reported effect size.","strength":"moderate","defeaters":["Corrected analysis still shows similar effect sizes","Independent replication of results"]},{"type":"presupposes","premiseIndexes":[4],"conclusionIndex":5,"warrant":"The statement that nuts are energy‑dense underlies the concern that their consumption could increase total caloric intake.","strength":"strong","defeaters":["Portion control can mitigate caloric impact","Nuts' satiety effects may offset energy density"]}]}
